title: Find the Longest Word in Dictionary That Is a Subsequence
description: Given s and dictionary words, return the longest word that is a subsequence of s. Example: abpcplea, dict=[ale, apple, monkey, plea] -> apple

examples.explanation: Initialize result=''. Check 'ale': i=0,j=0 s[0]='a' matches 'a', j=1; i=1 s[1]='b' no match; i=2 s[2]='p' no match; i=3 s[3]='c' no match; i=4 s[4]='p' no match; i=5 s[5]='l' matches 'l', j=2; i=6 s[6]='e' matches 'e', j=3 (all matched). Valid. result='ale' (len=3). Check 'apple': i=0,j=0 s[0]='a' matches 'a', j=1; i=1 s[1]='b' no match; i=2 s[2]='p' matches 'p', j=2; i=3 s[3]='c' no match; i=4 s[4]='p' matches 'p', j=3; i=5 s[5]='l' matches 'l', j=4; i=6 s[6]='e' matches 'e', j=5 (all matched). Valid. len=5 > 3, result='apple'. Check 'monkey': i=0,j=0 s[0]='a' no match; ... traverse all s, never find 'm', invalid. Check 'plea': i=0,j=0 s[0]='a' no match; i=1 s[1]='b' no match; i=2 s[2]='p' matches 'p', j=1; i=3 s[3]='c' no match; i=4 s[4]='p' no match; i=5 s[5]='l' matches 'l', j=2; i=6 s[6]='e' matches 'e', j=3; i=7 s[7]='a' matches 'a', j=4 (all matched). Valid but len=4 < 5, keep 'apple'. Return 'apple'.

Initialize result=''. Check 'a': valid (find 'a' at index 0), result='a'. Check 'ab': i=0,j=0 s[0]='a' matches 'a', j=1; i=1 s[1]='b' matches 'b', j=2 (all matched). Valid, len=2 > 1, result='ab'. Check 'abc': i=0,j=0 'a' match j=1; i=1 'b' match j=2; i=2 'c' match j=3. Valid, len=3 > 2, result='abc'. Check 'abcd': i=0 'a' match j=1; i=1 'b' match j=2; i=2 'c' match j=3; i=3 'd' match j=4. Valid, len=4 > 3, result='abcd'. Check 'bcd': valid but len=3 < 4, keep 'abcd'. Return 'abcd'.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n * m * l)
approaches.summary: For each word in the dictionary, check if it is a subsequence of s by iterating through both strings. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs. Use it only for understanding the problem or small datasets.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m * l), where n is length of s, m is number of words, and l is average word length

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Subsequence Check for Longest Dictionary Word
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking each dictionary word against string s to find the longest valid subsequence.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the problem. The intuition is straightforward: we need to find the longest word from the dictionary that appears as a subsequence in string s. A subsequence means the characters appear in the same order (not necessarily contiguous) in s. The brute force approach works by checking every dictionary word individually against s. For each word, we use two pointers: one traversing s and one traversing the word. We move through s looking for each character of the word in order. If we find all characters of the word in s in the correct order, it's a valid subsequence. We keep track of the longest valid word found, and if there are ties, we return the lexicographically smallest one. The algorithm: 1. Initialize result as empty string. 2. For each word in dictionary: a. Check if word is subsequence of s using two-pointer method. b. If valid AND (longer than current result OR same length but lexicographically smaller), update result. 3. Return result. Time complexity: O(n * m * l) where n = length(s), m = number of words, l = average word length. Each word check takes O(n) worst case as we traverse s once per word. Space complexity: O(1) excluding input storage. Connection to L1: This level uses the subsequence checking function from L1 as its core subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling ties correctly (need lexicographically smallest), not resetting pointers for each word check, inefficiently re-checking s from beginning for each character. Real code pattern: result = ''; for word in dictionary: if is_subsequence(s, word): if len(word) > len(result) or (len(word) == len(result) and word < result): result = word; return result.

examples.trace: Initialize result=''. Check 'ale': i=0,j=0 s[0]='a' matches 'a', j=1; i=1 s[1]='b' no match; i=2 s[2]='p' no match; i=3 s[3]='c' no match; i=4 s[4]='p' no match; i=5 s[5]='l' matches 'l', j=2; i=6 s[6]='e' matches 'e', j=3 (all matched). Valid. result='ale' (len=3). Check 'apple': i=0,j=0 s[0]='a' matches 'a', j=1; i=1 s[1]='b' no match; i=2 s[2]='p' matches 'p', j=2; i=3 s[3]='c' no match; i=4 s[4]='p' matches 'p', j=3; i=5 s[5]='l' matches 'l', j=4; i=6 s[6]='e' matches 'e', j=5 (all matched). Valid. len=5 > 3, result='apple'. Check 'monkey': i=0,j=0 s[0]='a' no match; ... traverse all s, never find 'm', invalid. Check 'plea': i=0,j=0 s[0]='a' no match; i=1 s[1]='b' no match; i=2 s[2]='p' matches 'p', j=1; i=3 s[3]='c' no match; i=4 s[4]='p' no match; i=5 s[5]='l' matches 'l', j=2; i=6 s[6]='e' matches 'e', j=3; i=7 s[7]='a' matches 'a', j=4 (all matched). Valid but len=4 < 5, keep 'apple'. Return 'apple'.

Initialize result=''. Check 'a': valid (find 'a' at index 0), result='a'. Check 'ab': i=0,j=0 s[0]='a' matches 'a', j=1; i=1 s[1]='b' matches 'b', j=2 (all matched). Valid, len=2 > 1, result='ab'. Check 'abc': i=0,j=0 'a' match j=1; i=1 'b' match j=2; i=2 'c' match j=3. Valid, len=3 > 2, result='abc'. Check 'abcd': i=0 'a' match j=1; i=1 'b' match j=2; i=2 'c' match j=3; i=3 'd' match j=4. Valid, len=4 > 3, result='abcd'. Check 'bcd': valid but len=3 < 4, keep 'abcd'. Return 'abcd'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking if One String is Subsequence of Another
ladders.desc: Function that determines if a word is a subsequence of string s using two-pointer technique.
ladders.explanation: This is the core subroutine used in the brute force solution. The intuition: to check if word is a subsequence of s, we need to verify that all characters of word appear in s in the same order, though not necessarily consecutively. We can do this efficiently with two pointers: one (i) traversing s and one (j) traversing word. We move through s sequentially, and whenever we find a character matching word[j], we advance j. If we reach the end of word (j == len(word)), all characters were found in order. If we reach the end of s first, the word is not a subsequence. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize i=0 (for s), j=0 (for word). 2. While i < len(s) and j < len(word): a. If s[i] == word[j], increment j (found this character). b. Always increment i (move through s). 3. Return j == len(word) (all characters found). Time: O(n) where n = length(s) in worst case. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called for each dictionary word in the full solution. Common mistakes: Not incrementing i when characters don't match (must always move through s), incorrect termination conditions, off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: def is_subsequence(s, word): i,j=0,0; while i<len(s) and j<len(word): if s[i]==word[j]: j+=1; i+=1; return j==len(word).

examples.trace: i=0,j=0: s[0]='a', word[0]='a' match → j=1; i=1. i=1,j=1: s[1]='b', word[1]='p' no match; i=2. i=2,j=1: s[2]='p', word[1]='p' match → j=2; i=3. i=3,j=2: s[3]='c', word[2]='p' no match; i=4. i=4,j=2: s[4]='p', word[2]='p' match → j=3; i=5. i=5,j=3: s[5]='l', word[3]='l' match → j=4; i=6. i=6,j=4: s[6]='e', word[4]='e' match → j=5; i=7. j=5==len('apple')=5 → return true.

i=0,j=0: s[0]='a', word[0]='m' no match; i=1. i=1,j=0: s[1]='b', word[0]='m' no match; i=2. i=2,j=0: s[2]='p', word[0]='m' no match; i=3. i=3,j=0: s[3]='c', word[0]='m' no match; i=4. i=4,j=0: s[4]='p', word[0]='m' no match; i=5. i=5,j=0: s[5]='l', word[0]='m' no match; i=6. i=6,j=0: s[6]='e', word[0]='m' no match; i=7. i=7,j=0: s[7]='a', word[0]='m' no match; i=8 (end). j=0 != len('monkey')=6 → return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Pointer Sequence Matching
ladders.desc: Technique of using two indices to traverse two sequences simultaneously to find ordered matches.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the two-pointer technique for matching sequences, which is the core logic behind subsequence checking. The intuition: when we need to verify that one sequence appears within another in order, we can use two pointers to traverse both sequences efficiently. One pointer moves through the main sequence (s), while the other moves through the target sequence (word) only when we find matches. This ensures we check characters in the correct order without backtracking. The key insight is that we always advance the pointer in the main sequence, but only advance the target pointer when we find a match. This works because if a character appears later in s, we will encounter it as we continue scanning. Step-by-step: Initialize pointers i=0 for s, j=0 for word. Loop while both pointers are within bounds: compare s[i] and word[j]; if equal, increment j (found a match); always increment i. After loop, if j reached end of word, all characters were found in order. Time: O(n) linear. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This technique is implemented in the is_subsequence function. Common mistakes: Trying to use nested loops (O(n²)), not understanding that we must traverse s linearly, confusion about when to move pointers. Real code pattern: while i < n and j < m: if s[i] == word[j]: j += 1; i += 1.

examples.trace: i=0,j=0: s[0]='a', word[0]='a' match → j=1; i=1. i=1,j=1: s[1]='b', word[1]='c' no match; i=2. i=2,j=1: s[2]='c', word[1]='c' match → j=2; i=3. i=3,j=2: s[3]='d', word[2]='e' no match; i=4. i=4,j=2: s[4]='e', word[2]='e' match → j=3; i=5. j=3==len('ace') → true.

i=0,j=0: s[0]='a', word[0]='a' match → j=1; i=1. i=1,j=1: s[1]='b', word[1]='e' no match; i=2. i=2,j=1: s[2]='c', word[1]='e' no match; i=3. i=3,j=1: s[3]='d', word[1]='e' no match; i=4. i=4,j=1: s[4]='e', word[1]='e' match → j=2; i=5. Loop ends (i=5). j=2 != len('aec')=3 → false (never found 'c' after 'e').

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Characters in Two Strings
ladders.desc: Iterating through two strings simultaneously and comparing characters at current positions.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves the fundamental operation of traversing two strings together and performing comparisons. The intuition: many string algorithms require examining characters from two strings in tandem. We need to manage two indices, ensure we don't go out of bounds, and compare characters at those positions. This builds toward the two-pointer technique. The key is understanding how to control two separate loops or use a single loop with careful index management. Step-by-step: Initialize two indices i and j to 0. Use a while loop that continues as long as both indices are within their string lengths. Inside the loop, access s[i] and word[j], compare them, and take appropriate action (like incrementing j if they match). Always increment i to move through s. The loop terminates when we've exhausted one string. Time: O(min(n,m)) typically. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This is the implementation detail of the two-pointer matching. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check array bounds, mixing up which index to increment, off-by-one errors in loop conditions. Real code pattern: i=0; j=0; while i < len(s) and j < len(word): # compare s[i] and word[j]; i += 1.

examples.trace: i=0,j=0: compare s[0]='c' and word[0]='a' → not equal. i=1,j=0: compare s[1]='a' and word[0]='a' → equal. i=2,j=1: compare s[2]='t' and word[1]='c' → not equal. End (i=3).
i=0,j=0: compare s[0]='d' and word[0]='g' → not equal. i=1,j=0: compare s[1]='o' and word[0]='g' → not equal. i=2,j=0: compare s[2]='g' and word[0]='g' → equal. i=3,j=1: loop ends (i out of bounds).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing String Characters by Index
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of retrieving a character from a string using its position.
ladders.explanation: This is a basic programming operation: accessing individual characters in a string by their index. The intuition: strings are sequences of characters, and we can refer to each character by its position (index), starting from 0. This is essential for any algorithm that processes strings character by character. In most programming languages, you can use array-like notation (e.g., s[i]) to get the character at position i. It's important to understand that indices must be within the valid range (0 to length-1) to avoid errors. Step-by-step: Given a string s and an integer i, check if i is between 0 and len(s)-1. If valid, access s[i] to get the character. This operation is typically O(1) time. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly when comparing characters between two strings. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing (should be 0-based), accessing out-of-bounds indices, confusing string length with last index. Real code pattern: char = s[i] where 0 <= i < len(s).

examples.trace: String 'hello' has indices: 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'. Accessing index 0 returns 'h'.
String 'world' indices: 0='w', 1='o', 2='r', 3='l', 4='d'. Index 4 is valid (len=5, so max index=4). Access returns 'd'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a String?
ladders.desc: Understanding strings as sequences of characters and basic string properties.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: understanding what a string is in programming. A string is a data type that represents a sequence of characters, such as letters, digits, or symbols. It's essentially an ordered collection where each element is a character. Key properties: 1. Strings have a length (number of characters). 2. Characters have positions called indices, starting from 0. 3. Strings can be empty (length 0). 4. Strings are typically immutable in many languages (cannot change individual characters directly). The intuition: before we can manipulate strings, we need to understand their basic structure. This concept is prerequisite to accessing characters, comparing strings, or iterating through them. Step-by-step: Consider a string like 'hello'. It has length 5. The characters are: position 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'. We can think of it as an array of characters. Time/space: Not applicable for this conceptual level. Connection to L4: This foundation enables understanding how to access characters by index. Common mistakes: Confusing strings with lists of words, misunderstanding 0-based indexing, not recognizing empty string as valid. Real code pattern: In Python, s = 'hello'; len(s) gives 5; s[0] gives 'h'.

examples.trace: The string 'cat' consists of 3 characters. Index 0 is 'c', index 1 is 'a', index 2 is 't'. The length is 3.
This is an empty string with no characters. Its length is 0. There are no indices to access.

approaches.name: Two Pointer Per Word O(n * m)
approaches.summary: For each dictionary word, use two pointers to traverse s and the word simultaneously to check subsequence status. This improves over brute force by avoiding nested loops within each word check. It is efficient for moderate-sized inputs.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m), where n is length of s and m is number of words

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Longest Dictionary Word That Is a Subsequence
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two-pointer per word approach to find the longest dictionary word that is a subsequence of string s.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the longest word from a dictionary that appears as a subsequence in string s. A subsequence means the characters appear in the same order (not necessarily contiguous). The two-pointer per word approach works by checking each dictionary word individually against string s. For each word, we use two pointers: one traversing s and one traversing the word. We move the s-pointer forward to find matches for the word's characters. If we successfully match all characters of the word, it's a valid subsequence. We track the longest valid word, breaking ties by lexicographical order. The intuition is that by processing each word independently with two pointers, we avoid the exponential complexity of checking all possible subsequences. The algorithm iterates through all words (m words), and for each word, traverses s (length n) once, giving O(n*m) time complexity. Space is O(1) excluding input storage. This approach is optimal for moderate inputs where m is not too large. Common mistakes include: not resetting pointers for each word, not handling empty inputs, and incorrect tie-breaking. The connection to L1 is that this level uses the subsequence checker as a helper function.

examples.trace: Initialize longest=''. For word='ale': i=0,j=0 (s[0]='a' matches 'a') → j=1; i=1 (s[1]='b' no match) → i=2; i=2 (s[2]='p' no match) → i=3; i=3 (s[3]='c' no match) → i=4; i=4 (s[4]='p' no match) → i=5; i=5 (s[5]='l' matches 'l') → j=2; i=6 (s[6]='e' matches 'e') → j=3 (end). j==len(word)=3 → valid. Compare: len('ale')=3 > len('')=0 → longest='ale'. For word='apple': i=0,j=0 (s[0]='a' matches 'a') → j=1; i=1 (s[1]='b' no match) → i=2; i=2 (s[2]='p' matches 'p') → j=2; i=3 (s[3]='c' no match) → i=4; i=4 (s[4]='p' matches 'p') → j=3; i=5 (s[5]='l' matches 'l') → j=4; i=6 (s[6]='e' matches 'e') → j=5 (end). Valid. Compare: len('apple')=5 > len('ale')=3 → longest='apple'. For word='monkey': i=0,j=0 (s[0]='a' no match) → i=1; ... i=7 (end) j=0 → invalid. For word='plea': i=0,j=0 (s[0]='a' no match) → i=1; ... i=4 (s[4]='p' matches 'p') → j=1; i=5 (s[5]='l' matches 'l') → j=2; i=6 (s[6]='e' matches 'e') → j=3; i=7 (s[7]='a' matches 'a') → j=4 (end). Valid. Compare: len('plea')=4 < len('apple')=5 → keep 'apple'. Return 'apple'.

Initialize longest=''. For word='ace': i=0,j=0 (s[0]='a' matches 'a') → j=1; i=1 (s[1]='b' no match) → i=2; i=2 (s[2]='c' matches 'c') → j=2; i=3 (s[3]='d' no match) → i=4; i=4 (s[4]='e' matches 'e') → j=3 (end). Valid. longest='ace'. For word='acd': i=0,j=0 (s[0]='a' matches 'a') → j=1; i=1 (s[1]='b' no match) → i=2; i=2 (s[2]='c' matches 'c') → j=2; i=3 (s[3]='d' matches 'd') → j=3 (end). Valid. Compare: len('acd')=3 == len('ace')=3, lexicographically 'acd' < 'ace' → longest='acd'. For word='abc': i=0,j=0 (s[0]='a' matches 'a') → j=1; i=1 (s[1]='b' matches 'b') → j=2; i=2 (s[2]='c' matches 'c') → j=3 (end). Valid. Compare: len('abc')=3 == len('acd')=3, lexicographically 'abc' < 'acd' → longest='abc'. For word='a': i=0,j=0 (s[0]='a' matches 'a') → j=1 (end). Valid. Compare: len('a')=1 < len('abc')=3 → keep 'abc'. Return 'abc'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check If a Word Is a Subsequence of String s
ladders.desc: Helper function that determines if a given word is a subsequence of string s using two pointers.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the full solution. Given string s and a word, it checks if all characters of the word appear in s in the same order (subsequence). The two-pointer technique uses pointer i for s and j for the word. We iterate through s with i, and whenever s[i] matches word[j], we advance j. If j reaches the end of the word, it's a valid subsequence. The intuition is that by scanning s linearly and matching characters in order, we efficiently verify the subsequence property without generating all combinations. Time complexity is O(n) where n is length of s, as we traverse s once. Space is O(1). Common mistakes: not handling empty word (should return True), and off-by-one errors in pointer updates. This feeds into L0 by being called for each dictionary word. Real code pattern: def is_subsequence(s, word): i=0; j=0; while i<len(s) and j<len(word): if s[i]==word[j]: j+=1; i+=1; return j==len(word).

examples.trace: i=0,j=0: s[0]='a' matches word[0]='a' → j=1; i=1. i=1,j=1: s[1]='b' no match → i=2. i=2,j=1: s[2]='p' matches word[1]='p' → j=2; i=3. i=3,j=2: s[3]='c' no match → i=4. i=4,j=2: s[4]='p' matches word[2]='p' → j=3; i=5. i=5,j=3: s[5]='l' matches word[3]='l' → j=4; i=6. i=6,j=4: s[6]='e' matches word[4]='e' → j=5; i=7. j=5==len(word)=5 → return True.

i=0,j=0: s[0]='a' matches word[0]='a' → j=1; i=1. i=1,j=1: s[1]='b' no match → i=2. i=2,j=1: s[2]='c' matches word[1]='c' → j=2; i=3. i=3,j=2: s[3]='d' no match → i=4. i=4,j=2: s[4]='e' matches word[2]='e' → j=3; i=5. j=3==len(word)=3 → return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Pointer Technique for Ordered Matching
ladders.desc: Using two pointers to traverse two sequences simultaneously to find ordered matches.
ladders.explanation: This technique involves using two indices (pointers) to iterate through two sequences (e.g., strings) to check if one is a subsequence of the other. Pointer i moves through the main string s, and pointer j moves through the target word. We advance i to scan s, and whenever s[i] matches word[j], we advance j. The key insight is that by moving i forward regardless of matches, we ensure we're checking in order, and by only advancing j on matches, we verify all characters of word appear in s in sequence. This is efficient because it processes each sequence linearly. Time O(n+m) but dominated by n since m≤n in subsequence check. Space O(1). Common mistakes: advancing j without match or resetting pointers incorrectly. This is the foundation for L1's subsequence checker. Real code: i=0; j=0; while i<len(s): if j<len(word) and s[i]==word[j]: j+=1; i+=1; return j==len(word).

examples.trace: Initialize i=0,j=0. Step1: i=0,j=0: s[0]='a' matches word[0]='a' → j=1; i=1. Step2: i=1,j=1: s[1]='b' no match → i=2. Step3: i=2,j=1: s[2]='c' matches word[1]='c' → j=2; i=3. Loop ends as i=3. j=2==len(word)=2 → True.

Initialize i=0,j=0. Step1: i=0,j=0: s[0]='a' no match with word[0]='b' → i=1. Step2: i=1,j=0: s[1]='b' matches word[0]='b' → j=1; i=2. Step3: i=2,j=1: s[2]='c' no match with word[1]='a' → i=3. Loop ends. j=1 != len(word)=2 → False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Strings with Index Pointers
ladders.desc: Using index variables to traverse strings and access characters sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This concept involves using integer indices (pointers) to loop through strings, accessing characters by position. For a string s, we use an index i starting at 0, increment it in a loop, and access s[i] to get each character. This is fundamental for processing sequences in algorithms. The intuition is that strings are arrays of characters, and indices allow linear traversal. We combine this with conditions (e.g., while i < len(s)) to avoid out-of-bounds errors. Time O(n) for traversing length n string. Space O(1). Common mistakes: forgetting to increment index or using wrong loop bounds. This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to move pointers. Real code: i=0; while i<len(s): char = s[i]; i+=1.

examples.output: Characters: h e l l o
examples.trace: Initialize i=0. Step1: i=0, s[0]='h' → process 'h'; i=1. Step2: i=1, s[1]='e' → process 'e'; i=2. Step3: i=2, s[2]='l' → process 'l'; i=3. Step4: i=3, s[3]='l' → process 'l'; i=4. Step5: i=4, s[4]='o' → process 'o'; i=5. Loop ends as i=5==len(s).
Initialize i=0. Step1: i=0, access s[0]='a' → index 0 char 'a'; i=1. Step2: i=1, access s[1]='b' → index 1 char 'b'; i=2. Step3: i=2, access s[2]='c' → index 2 char 'c'; i=3. Loop ends.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Characters in Strings
ladders.desc: Checking if two characters are equal, a fundamental operation in string processing.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves comparing two characters (single letters, digits, etc.) for equality. In programming, characters are often represented as strings of length 1 or char types. The comparison uses the == operator to check if they are the same. For example, 'a' == 'a' is True, 'a' == 'b' is False. This is essential for algorithms that match patterns in strings. The intuition is that characters are basic units, and equality checks drive decision-making in loops. Time O(1), space O(1). Common mistakes: comparing strings vs characters incorrectly or case sensitivity issues. This feeds into L3 by being used within loops to conditionally advance pointers. Real code: if char1 == char2: do_something().

examples.trace: Take char1='a' and char2='a'. Compare using ==: 'a' == 'a' evaluates to True. No further steps.
Take char1='x' and char2='y'. Compare using ==: 'x' == 'y' evaluates to False. That's it.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What Is a String and Character?
ladders.desc: Understanding strings as sequences of characters, the basic data type for text.
ladders.explanation: A string is a data type that represents a sequence of characters, such as letters, digits, or symbols. Characters are individual elements, e.g., 'a', 'b', '1'. Strings are ordered, meaning the position matters (e.g., 'ab' is different from 'ba'). They are typically immutable in many languages. The intuition is that strings allow us to store and process text. Operations include accessing characters by index (e.g., s[0] gives first character) and getting length. This is foundational for all string algorithms. Time to access a character is O(1), space depends on length. Common mistakes: confusing strings with lists or not understanding zero-based indexing. This feeds into L4 by providing the objects for comparison. Real code: s = 'hello'; char = s[0] (gets 'h'); len_s = len(s) (gets 5).

examples.trace: Given string 'cat'. It has 3 characters. Position 0: 'c', position 1: 'a', position 2: 't'. The length is 3. No operations needed, just identification.
String '123' is a sequence of digit characters. Position 0: '1', position 1: '2', position 2: '3'. Length is 3. Each element is a character.

approaches.name: Preprocess s with Next Array O(n * alphabet + m * l)
approaches.summary: Preprocess s to create a next array that stores the next occurrence of each character after each position, enabling O(l) subsequence checks per word. This is optimal for many queries on the same s. Use it when the dictionary is large or s is fixed.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * |alphabet| + m * l), where |alphabet| is the character set size (e.g., 26 for lowercase English)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Longest Dictionary Subsequence Using Next Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution that preprocesses string s with next array to efficiently find longest dictionary word that's a subsequence.
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the problem by first preprocessing the input string s to create a 'next array' data structure that enables O(l) subsequence checking for each dictionary word, where l is the word length. The intuition is that for many queries (dictionary words) on the same s, we can trade preprocessing time for faster per-word checking. The next array is a 2D array where next[i][c] stores the index of the next occurrence of character c starting from position i in s (or -1 if no such occurrence exists). After building this array in O(n * |alphabet|) time, we check each dictionary word by using the next array to 'jump' through s, verifying if all characters appear in order. We track the longest valid word, breaking ties lexicographically. The algorithm: 1. Build next array from s. 2. Initialize result as empty string. 3. For each word in dictionary: a. Check if word is subsequence using next array. b. If valid, compare with current result (longer wins, same length uses lexicographic order). 4. Return result. Time: O(n * |alphabet| + m * l) where n = |s|, m = dictionary size, l = average word length. Space: O(n * |alphabet|) for next array. Connection to L1: Uses the subsequence checking function as key subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling -1 correctly in next array (means character not found), forgetting to initialize next array properly, not considering lexicographic tie-breaking. Real pattern: Build next array backward from end of s, then check words by maintaining current position in s.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build next array for s='abpcplea' (length 8, alphabet lowercase). Initialize next[8][all chars] = -1. Process backward: i=7 (char 'a'): next[7]['a']=7, others copy from next[8]. i=6 ('e'): next[6]['e']=6, 'a' copies 7. Continue to i=0. Step 2: Check words. Start result=''. Word 'ale': Start pos=0. char 'a': next[0]['a']=0, pos=0+1=1. 'l': next[1]['l']=3, pos=3+1=4. 'e': next[4]['e']=6, pos=6+1=7. All found, valid. Compare: 'ale' (len 3) vs '' -> update to 'ale'. Word 'apple': pos=0. 'a': next[0]['a']=0, pos=1. 'p': next[1]['p']=2, pos=3. 'p': next[3]['p']=5, pos=6. 'l': next[6]['l']=-1 -> invalid. Actually correction: 'apple' chars: a,p,p,l,e. After 'a' at 0, 'p' at 2, second 'p' at 5, 'l' at next[6]['l']= -1? Wait, check: s indices: 0:a,1:b,2:p,3:c,4:p,5:l,6:e,7:a. After 'p' at 5 (index 4), pos=5, 'l': next[5]['l']=5, pos=6, 'e': next[6]['e']=6, valid. So 'apple' valid, len 5 > 3, update to 'apple'. Word 'monkey': 'm' not in s, invalid. Word 'plea': valid but len 4 < 5. Final result 'apple'.

Step 1: Build next array for s='abc' (n=3). Initialize next[3][all]=-1. i=2 ('c'): next[2]['c']=2. i=1 ('b'): next[1]['b']=1, 'c' copies 2. i=0 ('a'): next[0]['a']=0, others copy from next[1]. Step 2: Check words. Result=''. 'a': valid, update. 'ab': pos=0, 'a' at 0, pos=1, 'b' at 1, valid, len 2 > 1, update. 'abc': pos=0, 'a' at 0, pos=1, 'b' at 1, pos=2, 'c' at 2, valid, len 3 > 2, update. 'abcd': 'd' not in s, invalid. Final 'abc'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Subsequence Using Next Array
ladders.desc: Function that uses precomputed next array to check if a word is subsequence of s in O(l) time.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the efficient solution. Given a precomputed next array for string s and a word w, it determines if w is a subsequence of s by 'jumping' through s using the next array. The intuition is that the next array allows us to quickly find the next occurrence of any character from any starting position, avoiding linear scans through s for each character. The algorithm: 1. Initialize current position pos = 0 (starting index in s). 2. For each character c in w: a. Look up next[pos][c] from the array. b. If value is -1, return False (character not found after current position). c. Otherwise, update pos = next[pos][c] + 1 (move to position after this occurrence). 3. If all characters processed, return True. Time: O(l) where l = |w|, since each character requires constant-time array lookup. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This function is called for each dictionary word in the full solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to add 1 to pos after finding a character (to move past it), incorrect handling of pos=0 start, assuming next array indexes start at 1. Real pattern: Iterate through word, use next array to find positions, break if -1 encountered.

examples.trace: Assume next array built as in L0 example. Start pos=0. char 'a': next[0]['a']=0, pos=0+1=1. char 'p': next[1]['p']=2, pos=2+1=3. char 'p': next[3]['p']=5, pos=5+1=6. char 'l': next[6]['l']=-1? Wait, check s: indices 0:a,1:b,2:p,3:c,4:p,5:l,6:e,7:a. Actually, after second 'p' at index 4 (pos=5), next[5]['l']=5, pos=5+1=6. char 'e': next[6]['e']=6, pos=6+1=7. All chars found, return True.

Next array as in L0 example. Start pos=0. char 'a': next[0]['a']=0, pos=1. char 'b': next[1]['b']=1, pos=2. char 'c': next[2]['c']=2, pos=3. char 'd': next[3]['d']=-1 (since no 'd' in s), return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Building Next Array for Fast Character Lookup
ladders.desc: Preprocess string s to create array storing next occurrence of each character from each position.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique that enables efficient subsequence checking. The next array is a 2D array of size (n+1) × |alphabet|, where n = |s|. For position i (0 to n) and character c, next[i][c] stores the smallest index j >= i such that s[j] == c, or -1 if no such j exists. The intuition is that by precomputing this, we can answer 'where does character c appear next from position i?' in O(1) time. We build it backward for efficiency: start from i = n (end of string), where all next[n][c] = -1. Then for i from n-1 down to 0: copy next[i+1] to next[i], then set next[i][s[i]] = i (since at position i, character s[i] occurs at i). This works because when moving backward, we update the occurrence for the current character, and other characters inherit the next occurrence from the later position. Time: O(n * |alphabet|) to initialize and fill array. Space: O(n * |alphabet|). Connection to L1: This array is passed to the subsequence checking function. Common mistakes: Using forward construction which is less efficient, incorrect array dimensions (need n+1 to handle starting from position n), not initializing base case properly. Real pattern: Create array, set last row to -1, loop backward, copy row, update current character.

examples.trace: n=3, alphabet size 26 (lowercase). Initialize next[4][26]. Set next[3][all] = -1. i=2: s[2]='c'. Copy next[3] to next[2]: all -1. Set next[2]['c']=2. i=1: s[1]='b'. Copy next[2] to next[1]: next[1]['c']=2, others -1. Set next[1]['b']=1. i=0: s[0]='a'. Copy next[1] to next[0]: next[0]['b']=1, next[0]['c']=2, others -1. Set next[0]['a']=0. Result: next[0]['a']=0,['b']=1,['c']=2; next[1]['b']=1,['c']=2; next[2]['c']=2; next[3] all -1.

n=2. Initialize next[3][26], next[2][all]=-1. i=1: s[1]='a'. Copy next[2] to next[1]: all -1. Set next[1]['a']=1. i=0: s[0]='a'. Copy next[1] to next[0]: next[0]['a']=1 (from copy), others -1. Set next[0]['a']=0 (overwrites to 0). Result: next[0]['a']=0; next[1]['a']=1; next[2] all -1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Subsequence Checking with Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Check if a word is subsequence of s by scanning both strings linearly.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is the naive way to check subsequence, which helps understand why we need the optimized next array. A word w is a subsequence of s if we can delete some characters from s (without changing order) to get w. The intuition is to use two pointers: one for s and one for w, advancing through s to match characters of w in order. Algorithm: 1. Initialize i=0 (index in s), j=0 (index in w). 2. While i < |s| and j < |w|: a. If s[i] == w[j], increment j (matched a character). b. Always increment i (move forward in s). 3. If j == |w|, all characters matched, return True; else False. Time: O(n) where n=|s|, since we scan s once. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: The next array optimization reduces this from O(n) to O(l) per word by precomputing jump positions. Common mistakes: Not incrementing i when characters don't match, incorrect loop conditions, off-by-one errors. Real pattern: Two-pointer scan, match characters, check if all word chars consumed.

examples.trace: i=0,j=0: s[0]='a', w[0]='a', match, j=1, i=1. i=1,j=1: s[1]='b', w[1]='c', no match, i=2. i=2,j=1: s[2]='c', w[1]='c', match, j=2, i=3. i=3 end, j=2 < len=3, return False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Strings and Comparing Characters
ladders.desc: Fundamental skill of looping through strings and checking character equality.
ladders.explanation: This is a basic programming operation where you traverse a string character by character and compare characters with another string. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can access each character by index. Algorithm: Use a for-loop or while-loop with an index variable to iterate from 0 to length-1. At each position, access character using s[i] and compare with another character using ==. Time: O(n) for traversing string of length n. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used in the linear scan for subsequence checking. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds (e.g., accessing s[len(s)]), confusing string length with last index, not handling empty strings. Real pattern: for i in range(len(s)): char = s[i]; or use while loop with index.

examples.trace: Initialize i=0. s[0]='h', compare with 'e': false, increment i=1. s[1]='e', compare: true, stop and return true.

examples.input: s='abc', compare with 'abd' character by character
examples.trace: Loop i=0 to 2. i=0: s[0]='a', compare with 'a': true. i=1: s[1]='b', compare with 'b': true. i=2: s[2]='c', compare with 'd': false, so strings differ at index 2.

testCases.input: s='test', check if first char is 't'

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Subsequence?
ladders.desc: Understanding the definition of subsequence: a sequence derived by deleting characters without changing order.
ladders.explanation: A subsequence is a fundamental concept in computer science and mathematics. Given a string s, a subsequence is formed by taking some characters from s (possibly none) without changing the relative order of the remaining characters. Intuition: Think of it as a 'pattern' that appears in s in order, but not necessarily consecutively. For example, in s='abc', 'ac' is a subsequence because 'a' and 'c' appear in order (with 'b' skipped), but 'ca' is not because 'c' comes before 'a' in s. Key properties: 1. Every string is a subsequence of itself. 2. The empty string is a subsequence of any string. 3. If w is subsequence of s, then length(w) <= length(s). Algorithm to understand: Given s and w, can you point to characters in s that match w in order? Connection to L4: This concept uses character comparison to verify subsequence. Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence with substring (which requires consecutive characters), thinking order can change. Real pattern: Visualize by highlighting matching characters in s.

examples.trace: In s='abc', characters: a, b, c. To form 'ac', take 'a' (index 0), skip 'b', take 'c' (index 2). Order preserved: a before c. Yes.
s='a','p','p','l','e'. For 'ape': take 'a' (index 0), skip first 'p', take second 'p' (index 2) as 'p', take 'e' (index 4). Order: a, p, e matches. Yes.
